speaker: interviewer
text: We're beginning tape number 4, interview with Mr. Harry Schlesinger. It is March the 30th, 1997. Mr. Schlesinger, when we left off, you were mentioning that you had your own room where you were that you were actually able to live pretty well. Tell me a little bit about your experiences with Dr. Mengele himself. How often did you see him? And-- and what was he like?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, he was a good-looking German officer. When you looked at him, he looked to you like an angel. But he was the devil of the angels. He killed people without thinking, without feelings. And the world included America. He was here has twice-- in United States. He had a passport under his own name. And they let him go. It's a shame. It's unbelievable. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Not only he's protected and was protected. All these officers who run away to South America, they have meetings yearly in Germany. They get checks from Germany. They're helped out from their families from Germany. They are in contact. And this was all done-- if you ever was reading the book what an Irish man is writing here in America. And I would suggest every Jew to read, and even every non-Jew-- how we were treated in this country.

speaker: interviewer
text: Getting back to 1944--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in your time at Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about what you experienced with Dr. Mengele.

speaker: interviewee
text: Dr. Mengele, we had to attend to his office. There was a lager. They call it Zigeunerlager-- where the gypsies were there. His official officer was there. He had a woman-- if I'm not mistaken, a Belgian woman was his secretary, a Jewish woman. I would say about 20, 22 years old. When we had to get to his office by appointment, first we had to go to one of the showers in the Zigeunerlager, in the Gypsy lager, to take a shower to be clean. And then we had to be there by him. He had, from every twin, hundreds of documents, all the details. He looked in every little thing. He asked thousands of questions.

speaker: interviewer
text: We were undressed, completely naked. He started to measure us-- the finger, the nail, color of the eyes, color of the hair. What was the situation in our family? Who was sick? Who had any problems in life? All the details, all-- all the details. In fact, when he run away already from Birkenau because Russian came-- as the-- some of the guys told me. They were there, next to his office. The main thing-- he was looking for all of the papers to take with him from the twins. Because he was interested-- first of all, because he's doing a good work for the German nation, for the German government. At the same time, he himself wanted to know, actually, what's about his own twins?

speaker: interviewee
text: He's the one came to the decision that never a twin could have a pair of twins. Only second generation could have a twin. And he proved it with all his study. In fact, I am a twin with my sister. I never had a twin between my two children. My sister never had twins. But my sister's daughter has a twin. So it means he really brought it up that only the third generation could always have a twin.

speaker: interviewer
text: How frequently were you meeting with him while you were at Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Roughly speaking, I would say every second week. Hours, I used to spend with him. When we came to him, they gave us, in order we should tell more, we should feel that-- that he's our friend, that he wants to help us, he's our father-- [YIDDISH]. We got-- instead of a regular soup, we got a macaroni soup. Instead of black bread, we get white bread, like the Italian bread. And, you see, as he confused everybody-- as the German confused all the Jews, that's how he did it with us too. We-- he didn't give us the slightest thinking that-- that he want to destroy us. Or by the end, this whole thing is just a-- a game for him. But the way I found out-- because I used to be together with the doctors. I was in a barrack where there were Jewish doctors who were Polish doctors since 1939. And they told me all the experiments. When they told me-- when they looked at us, the twins, they-- they were human being. They still wasn't so hungry like the rest. When a person is hungry, when a person is really in trouble, he becomes automatically a wild animal. It's later I'm going to tell you what happened the last month-- the last days before being free in Mauthausen. Human Jewish being ate human beings. They cut out a piece of meat from your behind, and they ate it. They burned it, and they ate it. That's how a human being could become when he is hungry. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] A son was waiting the father should die already to take out his gold teeth. He should get a bite of bread for it. So we became animals. We are not-- we were not anymore human beings. And that's what the German nation brought it to it. I don't believe, in Africa, where they're eating human being, there could be such a sadistic people like the German nation were. And this is in their blood. I don't think so anybody could do this, included with the-- the Hungarians. They could do it. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: While you were actually at Auschwitz, what were you thinking about Mengele at that time? What was your opinion of him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Let me put it this way. In Auschwitz, nobody was thinking. In Auschwitz, everybody was only thinking about one thing-- how to survive the today, how to get a piece of bread, a little salt. This-- this was no such a thing-- thinking. Because if-- if a person would think, in the beginning today-- all these rich children who came from wealthy families. And when they started to think that tomorrow morning, my maid is not going to clean my shoes and my mother is not going to prepare me my food on the table, he would not survive anyway. He went over to the wire, hold the wire with the 5,000-volt electricity, and he took his life. Many, many people in the beginning. When they serve you the first time a soup-- you had to stay in the line. They didn't even give you a plate. They give you a-- a spoon, drink it out fast, fast, and the next guy was drinking it out.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you was biting this-- whatever it was in it, you felt more-- how do you call it? [GERMAN]? Little stones in the soup. You know, they was putting in soups, in order should be a heavier soup-- when you cut wood, how do you call it? Sawdust.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sawdust into the soup. They was putting in the bread-- the bread should be heavier. They didn't care about our health. They was looking for, every day, more and more people should die. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] There was no thinking. We became animals.

speaker: interviewer
text: In addition to taking all these measurements of you every-- every two weeks, did you have-- were there any other experiments conducted on you at any time?

speaker: interviewee
text: They took blood. These doctors whom I used to be together, in evening we used to talk. They told me about experiments. You wouldn't believe it. They took children. And they gave injection straight to the heart-- water injection. And they were standing with stop watches to check it. This child survives two minutes. This child survive three minutes.

speaker: interviewer
text: This kind of experiment was-- maybe it wasn't even necessary. Maybe it was just for fun. But what was a human being? Nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now about this week-- about the Gate of Heaven. We see there an article where this crazy guy, he was castrated. Now one of the castrating experiment-- I have to tell you-- because that's what made me remind. Because otherwise, I would forget. Hitler's plan was that he will win the war. And after the war, anybody who hasn't got the blonde hair with the blue eyes, everybody will be his slave. A slave, like a horse, who's going to work for Germany, use his power, his energy, and get the hell out from this world. This was his plan. Now you know very well, if a horse or any animal who's castrated has more power than without it. So he made an experiment. If it was 50, or 100 pair, I don't remember. That they-- what they told me, the doctors. They took Jewish young boys and girls, from age 18 up to 25. As I said, I don't remember if it's 50 or 100.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in order to make a castration, you need a hospital, you need a doctor, you need a nurse, you need all kind of facilities. It's too much money. For what? For a Jew, a-- a Jew who doesn't deserve even a bullet. They just need gas to destroy him what doesn't cost even a penny. So we'll make this operation with beautiful German engineering how to do this. You have million of peoples, slaves after the war. Maybe the colored people, maybe the Mongolian, whoever-- whoever not Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: But this cost a lot of money. So we have to prepare this already for after the war-- how to do this in mass production. And it shouldn't cost money.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they took a writing desk. First, the couple, the young boy, they had to make sure that they're sexually active, that they are healthy people. So they had to have, first, sex. Once they had it, they were sent to a writing desk. Under the writing desk was an x-ray machine. And with x-ray, how do-- 'waves,' they call it? 'Waves?' With x-ray waves was set up the waves that between the legs, to give me the-- the X should be burned out.

speaker: interviewee
text: All these boys and girls, they didn't survive. They all died. There is only one boy was still alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why was he alive? Because he had an uncle, a kapo, a foreman, a Jew, who, whenever was a selection and whenever they saw a struggling, sick boy-- between those boys and girls, he was hiding him aside. So he was hidden. I still saw one of him-- this boy. He was crying day and night. Why my uncle doesn't let me to die? I'm suffering. It hurts. It's-- that's the type experiments they made. Hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- they made shades from human-- from human skin. They still-- you can get, even today-- it's a big prize. You can get a piece of soap what it says, 'Reines Judisches Fett.' I will show you. I have a-- I'm sorry that I bring it to you. I will send this picture I have to send you. They made a special grave in Hungary in a certain town that all the soaps what were still left over are buried. And it's details-- where it was done, what date it was done, and where was it done. Soap from human beings.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think, in Africa, those cannibalism what were eating human being would do it? They wouldn't do it. Only these German, intelligent people did it. When you were in the-- when you were in twin block or while you were observing things going on in the twin block, were there ever any selections done there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Always. Always. But only Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: How often?

speaker: interviewee
text: The selection was always done-- twice was it done. I mean, two occasion it was done-- the selection. When the transport arrived was a selection. And when there was no transport, then they collected from all the factories, from all the-- that they-- coal mines. And they sended. The fact-- the crematorium had to work 24 hours. In Birkenau, what they called the Vernichtungslager, there was four crematoriums. And these had to work day and night, 24-hour shift with 200 speed. This had to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did the selections in the twin barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the twin-- in the-- no. It once happened to us that Mengele went to another town. And he was not there. And came another doctor who made-- his doctor name was Tillor, a German. And he was not informed, probably, that those are twin whom you're not supposed to make selections. And he made selection on the twins. But looks like our luck, came Mengele right away, dissolved this selection because he didn't finish yet his study. I mean, not because he likes us. It wasn't finished yet. He-- also he made experiments on midgets. In my barrack, what-- the same between the twins were midgets. There are all kind of midgets. In fact, there are two type of midgets. That's how I learned this-- in Auschwitz, you see. There is a midget who is proportion-- the hand, and the feet, and the nose, and everything proportioned small. But there is another type of midget. This is not a midget. This is a sickness that a person is born with a regular body, and the feet and the hand is short. When a midget-- this type of midget gives you the-- shakes your hand, he shakes you like this. That's how. It's a short hand, short legs. Between those six midgets were, from Romania, a family. And while he made the selection, he saw seven little people, and each one another instrument, when he made this selection. One is a harmonica. One is a violin. One-- you know. And they used to travel in Europe for every country in Europe, play a game-- I mean, a show. And the name was The Seven Midgets. When he saw those people, he was interested in every unusual thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Finally, he made the selection. He went over there. And he says, what-- what do you have? Because, each one-- his instrument in the hand. He says, we are a theater. And we play, each one, on another instrument. And how many are you? He says, we are nine children, but we are seven midgets. This went into his head. Now he will study, how is it possible that a father and a mother has 11-- 9 children, 7 midget and 2 normals? So first, he took them right away, put them aside. Had to continue the selection. It was very important, you understand. And figures-- when he finish his selection, he comes right away and takes the midget to the same barrack where he studied the rest of the studies.

speaker: interviewee
text: He finished the-- the-- the selection. And he comes over. There is no midgets there.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So came another SS with a truck. And if he saw midgets and he saw little people-- and they are not going to go to work. They're not going to dig things. So he took them to the crematorium.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he saw this, that the midgets are not there. He took-- he had a motorcycle-- and run over to the-- right away to the gas chamber. They were already under-dressed to get the gas already in the gas chamber. Do you know what it means? Not only he took them. He asked all of them, you have relatives here? In order to get from the relatives more information what's going on with this-- with this midget family.

speaker: interviewee
text: And a group of 30 people, women and men, were in this group. They were already in the gas chamber. They're still alive, a few, in Israel. And he gave-- this was the-- they say, the first time in history for Auschwitz when the gas was already on and they gave an order to stop the gas. He took them out and brought them to my barrack. To my barrack was taken only two. There were two men, midgets, and five women. They was with my sister in their barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find out about this entire story?

speaker: interviewee
text: We sat eight months together in the same barrack.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Every night we was talking. They was put in [INAUDIBLE] every morning. This gangster Mengele brought them in order to get more information-- brought them Friday candles. They should be able to light on candles for Shabbos.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hm. And they were allowed to own the tefillin as well? They were able to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah, he knew it, because he wanted to get all that information out of them. First of all, he couldn't understand, how the hell is it possible when nine children, two normals and-- and seven midgets?

speaker: interviewer
text: So he started to study and dig into deeper in-- in feelings. Maybe your mother had to do with another man. These two normal were from another man. And the seven midgets were-- were from another man. So until they proved it-- that their mother and their father-- strictly religious, who never had to do with any other man and not any woman. And they convinced him, you understand? He needed all these details. He asked us a lot about the-- the Torah-- all kind of religious question. How you feel about this? And, how you feel about this? You know, for what purpose, I cannot tell you. Hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: So what happened to this-- yeah, a funny thing. A midget could have a baby with a normal woman. We had one midget, between the two men, who had a wife, a wife from 6-foot tall.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And one midget who was a single guy. This single guy, he was young. I was, this time, 15 year old. All his stories-- he was single guy. Every time when he-- they made a show, he met other woman. It was crazy because he has a beautiful face, but a midget. So that's what he was studying on. Also, there was midgets. There was a normal midget-- really proportions small. One-- one from Slovakia, who was wearing a big cross. He probably converted. Or maybe his family converted. This guy was such a genius painter. The SS, they used to come, bring him chocolate and candies and fruit, everything. The SS used to sit down for three minutes, and he made a sketch from him. It was so original, like a regular pictures from a studio.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: One was a midget who was with two [INAUDIBLE]. He didn't survive. He was the only one who died there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Weeks he was studying on his body. Usually, if somebody died, next day, they took him to the-- or the day-- crematorium. There was always-- there was a [GERMAN], they call it, where they studied the bodies. What do you call it here in America?

speaker: interviewer
text: An autopsy.

speaker: interviewee
text: Autopsy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Weeks, he was studying this guy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Each time, this doctor told me, he's still not finished with his study.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm. You had mentioned Professor Epstein as being someone who was working under Dr. Mengele.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, he was his right hand.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any other doctors working under Mengele? And were they all German? Or what was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Jews, Jews. I don't know-- I don't know about Germans. I cannot tell you. But the Jews, I know were, plenty of them. In fact, my barrack was over 20 doctors.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Jews, yeah. All these under-commander who was feeding the crematorium, they were all mostly-- 80% doctors. Doctors, dentists, a few lawyers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. Were you, at any time while you were at Auschwitz, able to see or have contact with your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was that possible?

speaker: interviewee
text: How it was possible-- I'll explain it to you. End of '44, they-- first of all, there was a time when my sister-- between me and my sister was only one wire. Later on, they took her to another camp. So we could go to-- and to talk to each other. But later on, before the end, when they brought the last ghetto-- if I'm not mistaken, from Lodz, they brought a transport. And this transport came. And the SS, who were-- who brought the people from this Lodz ghetto, they were Russian Mongolian-- Russian soldiers. And they kept-- then they-- then they got to-- then the Germans took them in captivity. They was willing to fight with the-- die with-- with the Germans against the Russian. And they made from them SS soldiers. And when they saw already, the Germans, that the war is ending, the Russians are coming closer, those Mongolian Russian soldiers, who were dressed up in SS suits with guns, brought this transport from Lodz to Auschwitz. When they arrived, they took off their clothing, SS clothing. They got the same stripes what's like the rest of the Jews. But in order they were helping the Germans, they gave them a special job. And they call it the [GERMAN]. [GERMAN] means they had-- it was a little wagon where all the food was distributed from one camp to another. And they were pushing these little wagons with stripes. They had enough food. They had clothing. And they were also in the same barrack where we were, between the twins.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one day I see they're bringing in big, big sacks with [INAUDIBLE]. I said, what the-- what's going on here? They want to make us religious, [? bringing ?] in [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened-- they didn't have underwear because there was no such a thing in Auschwitz-- underwear. So but-- but they were still SS. And they still was fighting for the Germans. So they gave them the good wool [INAUDIBLE]. And they was cutting it and made socks from it and underwear.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what happened. They gave them everything to give them their satisfaction. Now, one day, I wanted to go there to the-- my sister. And I didn't know how to get there. And I-- I had all kind of stuff. So I gave them presents. We became friends. And they told me, if you want to go to the other-- because they used to bring the food to the-- the ladies. I said, I'll give you a nice present if you let me. So one day they gave me-- one of the guy-- his place. And instead of eight people should push the wagon, I was one of the eight. And that's how I got to my sister, to-- to the ladies' camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we're going end the tape here. And we'll pick this up with tape number 5.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.
